pick: every minute of the midnight hour
reasoning: "Midnight through 12:59 a.m." conflates two naming registers (midnight / a.m.) for the same hour boundary, creating an implicit redundancy. "The midnight hour" is a settled idiom that names exactly 00:00–00:59 without forcing the reader to reconstruct a range from a named instant to a clock time.

pick: every minute of the noon hour
reasoning: "From noon through 12:59 p.m." is directly redundant: noon is 12:00 p.m., so the phrase simultaneously uses a named instant and a clock/period suffix to label the same point, then appends 12:59 p.m. The mix of registers forces the reader to reconcile two systems. "The noon hour" is idiomatic, unambiguous, and eliminates the redundancy entirely.

pick: every minute of the 9 a.m. hour
reasoning: The range-span is technically correct, but for a single whole hour it synthesizes a range that conveys no additional precision — and repeating "a.m." at both endpoints adds clutter. The single-hour idiom is more economical and equally clear. (Range-span would be appropriate if the span were a subset or crossed an a.m./p.m. boundary.)

pick: every minute of the 5 p.m. hour
reasoning: Same logic as h9: "5 p.m. through 5:59 p.m." repeats "p.m." twice and adds no precision over the idiom for a single whole hour. The idiomatic form is cleaner and consistent with the other whole-hour entries.
